questions.prompt: What's the universal pattern every agent framework reduces to?

choices.text: A loop: call model → execute the tools it requested → append results to the conversation → repeat until the model says it's done (or a step cap fires). Every major framework — OpenAI Agents SDK, LangGraph, AutoGen, Anthropic tool use — is a wrapper around this loop.
A directed acyclic graph of LLM calls.

questions.explanation: Agents are stateless loops that put the model in charge of step selection. Frameworks add ergonomics (handlers, graphs, multi-agent primitives) on top, but the core is always: model picks a tool, you run it, you feed the result back, loop. Knowing the loop means you understand every framework.

questions.prompt: A production agent runs overnight in a fetch-retry-fetch loop, burning a 4-figure bill. What was missing?

choices.text: A step cap (maxSteps). Without one, a buggy agent or adversarial input can loop forever — and you pay per step. The right pattern: cap iterations (10-15 standard, 50 for deep research), treat hitting the cap as an explicit error, escalate or return partial. Pair with per-tool timeouts and daily spend caps.

questions.explanation: Step caps are non-negotiable in agent design. They convert 'unbounded loop' into 'explicit error you can handle.' Combined with timeouts and spend caps, they're the safety net that turns demo-grade agents into production-grade ones.
